name: Ariza and Embid Castles Loop
name_original: Cabolafuente - Embid de Ariza - Deza - Bordalba — circular desde Ariza
description: This difficult 47.7-mile road cycling route from Ariza explores Cabolafuente, Embid de Ariza, Deza, and Bordalba, climbing 3098 feet.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Ariza traverse a landscape characterized by rolling hills, river valleys, and historical landmarks. The region features varied terrain with significant elevation changes, offering routes that climb through rural areas and descend into scenic lowlands. Road cyclists can expect to encounter a mix of open countryside and routes passing near small villages. The area provides opportunities for extended rides across its diverse topography. Best road cycling routes around Ariza The most popular road cycling route is Jaraba-Calmarza Loop, a 37.6 miles (60.5 km) moderate trail that takes 2 hours 55 minutes to complete. This route offers a balanced ride through the local countryside. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Ariza and Embid Castles Loop, a difficult 47.7 miles (76.8 km) path. This route features significant climbs and passes by historical castle sites. Local road cyclists also love the Arcos de Jalón por la Cuerda de Villalpardo — circular desde Santa María de Huerta, a 44.3 miles (71.4 km) trail leading through elevated terrain, often completed in about 3 hours 18 minutes. Road cycling around Ariza is defined by rolling hills, river valleys, and historical points of interest. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate rides to more challenging routes with substantial elevation gain. The routes in Ariza are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.0 stars from over 3 reviews. More than 100 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Ariza's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes around Ariza?
A: Road cycling routes around Ariza traverse a landscape characterized by rolling hills, river valleys, and historical landmarks. You can expect varied terrain with significant elevation changes, offering routes that climb through rural areas and descend into scenic lowlands. The area provides opportunities for extended rides across its diverse topography.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners or those seeking a moderate challenge in Ariza?
A: While there are no routes specifically graded as 'easy', Ariza offers several moderate road cycling routes. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992996662/jaraba-calmarza-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Jaraba-Calmarza Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 37.6-mile (60.5 km) ride, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1077407144/arcos-de-jalon-por-la-cuerda-de-villalpardo-circular-desde-santa-maria-de-huerta" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Arcos de Jalón por la Cuerda de Villalpardo — circular desde Santa María de Huerta&lt;/a&gt; is another moderate 44.3-mile (71.4 km) option.

Q: Which road cycling routes offer a more challenging experience in Ariza?
A: For experienced road cyclists looking for a challenge, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992560985/ariza-and-embid-castles-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ariza and Embid Castles Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 47.7-mile (76.8 km) path featuring significant climbs and passing by historical castle sites. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20143126" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pozo Redondo Waterfall – Santuario de Jaraba loop from Alhama de Aragón&lt;/a&gt;, which is 50 miles (80.3 km) and includes substantial elevation gain.

Q: What is the typical length of road cycling routes around Ariza?
A: The road cycling routes in Ariza generally range from approximately 37 miles (60 km) to 53 miles (85 km) in length, offering substantial distances for extended rides.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes available in Ariza?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes around Ariza are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992996662/jaraba-calmarza-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Jaraba-Calmarza Loop&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992560985/ariza-and-embid-castles-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ariza and Embid Castles Loop&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1077407144/arcos-de-jalon-por-la-cuerda-de-villalpardo-circular-desde-santa-maria-de-huerta" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Arcos de Jalón por la Cuerda de Villalpardo — circular desde Santa María de Huerta&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What historical landmarks or scenic views can I expect to see along the road cycling routes?
A: Many routes offer scenic views and pass by historical points of interest. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992560985/ariza-and-embid-castles-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ariza and Embid Castles Loop&lt;/a&gt; takes you past castle sites like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7720996" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embid Castle&lt;/a&gt;. Other attractions in the wider area include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2529637" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monasterio de Santa María de Huerta&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2530043" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Thermal Lake of Alhama de Aragón&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about the routes in Ariza?
A: The road cycling routes in Ariza are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.0 stars from over 3 reviews. Cyclists often appreciate the varied terrain, the mix of open countryside, and the opportunities to pass near small villages and historical sites.

Q: Are there any routes that pass by waterfalls or other natural features?
A: Yes, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20143126" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pozo Redondo Waterfall – Santuario de Jaraba loop from Alhama de Aragón&lt;/a&gt; is a challenging route that specifically includes the Pozo Redondo Waterfall, offering a natural highlight along the ride.

Q: What is the best season for road cycling in Ariza?
A: While specific seasonal data isn't provided, the region's climate typically makes spring and autumn ideal for road cycling, offering milder temperatures. Summer can be hot, especially in the middle of the day, and winter might bring colder conditions, though routes remain accessible.

Q: Are there options for less crowded road cycling routes in Ariza?
A: Given the rural nature of the Ariza region, many routes offer a sense of open countryside and can be less crowded than more popular cycling destinations. Exploring routes that pass through smaller villages or more remote areas will likely provide a quieter experience.

Q: Are there any road cycling routes that connect to other towns or points of interest?
A: Routes often connect various towns and points of interest. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31502196" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Calatayud – ⭐️ Puerta de Terrer loop from Alhama de Aragón&lt;/a&gt; starts from Alhama de Aragón and takes you towards Calatayud, allowing you to explore different areas within the region.
tips: With the mission of defending the Kingdom of Aragon from the Kingdom of Castile, this Castle is located on top of a hill overlooking the town with the natural defense of the Henar River. All these castles in the Calatayud region were very important during the War of the Two Pedros. It is currently in ruins.
In this town are the remains of the Castillo de Ariza which, despite its current appearance, was the most important fortress in Aragon during the early fifteenth century.
Beautiful church built during the 16th century in the town of Ariza. Baroque in style and built in ashlar masonry, it was cataloged as an Inventory of Aragonese Cultural Heritage.

faqs.question: How long does it take to complete the Cabolafuente - Embid de Ariza - Deza - Bordalba circular road cycling route?
faqs.answer: This circular road cycling route is estimated to take approximately 3 hours and 41 minutes to complete. This duration is an estimate and can vary based on your cycling pace, stops, and overall fitness level.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route is classified as difficult for road cycling. It covers a distance of nearly 77 km and involves a significant elevation gain of over 944 meters. Cyclists should be prepared for challenging sections and sustained climbs.

faqs.question: Is this a loop trail or an out-and-back route?
faqs.answer: This is a circular route, meaning it starts and ends in the same general area, allowing you to return to your starting point without retracing your path.

faqs.question: Where is the best place to start and park for this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route is described as circular from Ariza. It is advisable to look for parking facilities within Ariza itself, as it serves as the primary starting and ending point for this loop. Specific trailhead parking details are not available, so checking local information upon arrival is recommended.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like for road cycling on this route?
faqs.answer: Given its location in the Aragon region, the terrain can vary. While some sections may feature well-paved surfaces, particularly minor or rural roads might have varying pavement quality. The route traverses areas that can involve mountainous or rugged terrain, typical of the Iberian System, so be prepared for diverse road conditions.

faqs.question: What can I see along the Cabolafuente - Embid de Ariza - Deza - Bordalba route?
faqs.answer: Along the route, you'll pass through several charming villages and experience the rural landscape. Notable highlights include the historic Embid Castle, Castillo de Ariza, and the Church of Santa María la Real de Ariza, offering glimpses into the region's rich history and architecture.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle in the Aragon region generally depends on avoiding extreme summer heat and winter cold. Spring (April-June) and Autumn (September-October) typically offer more moderate temperatures and pleasant conditions for road cycling.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While dogs are often welcome in outdoor areas, specific rules for road cycling routes can vary. On public roads, dogs are generally permitted if kept under control. However, for the comfort and safety of your pet and other cyclists, it's always best to ensure your dog is accustomed to cycling alongside you and to check local regulations in the villages you pass through.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee for this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this public road route. You are free to enjoy the open roads and villages along the way.

faqs.question: Are there cafes or accommodation options along the route?
faqs.answer: The route connects several villages, including Cabolafuente, Embid de Ariza, Deza, and Bordalba, as well as starting from Ariza. These villages may offer local cafes, restaurants, or small guesthouses. It's recommended to check availability and opening hours in advance, especially in smaller communities.

faqs.question: What should I pack for this road cycling trip?
faqs.answer: For this challenging road cycling route, pack essentials such as sufficient water and snacks, a repair kit for your bike, appropriate cycling attire for varying weather, sun protection, and a fully charged phone. Given the route's length and difficulty, consider carrying a basic first-aid kit and informing someone of your itinerary.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect with other well-known cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route briefly intersects with sections of the Camino del Cid (Senderista), the GR 160 - Camino del Cid BTT, and the Tierras de Frontera. These intersections are minimal, covering about 1% of the total route for each, but they indicate the presence of a wider network of trails in the region.
